label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the history experts' statement can prove about "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A through D. First, I need to look back at the passage. The history experts are mentioned in the paragraph that says, "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." So, the key points here are that there's no record of the original lyricists and that the song was being used by the public before a company owned it. That seems to relate to ownership. Warner Music Group claims copyright since 1988, but if the song was in public use before any company claimed it, then maybe Warner doesn't have the right. Looking at the options: A) does not belong to Warner Music Group. This seems plausible because if the song was in public domain long before Warner owned it, then their copyright claim might be invalid. B) has more than 200 years' history. The song was written in 1893 as "Good Morning to All," so about 120 years. The lyrics changed later. So 200 years is too long. B is probably wrong. C) was initially owned by another company. The passage doesn't mention another company initially owning it. The Hill sisters wrote the original song, but the lyrics were changed. The copyright is now claimed by Warner since 1988, but there's no mention of another company before that. So C might not be right. D) has always been very popular. The passage says it was being sung by the public long before being printed, but "always been popular" isn't directly supported. The experts talk about the history and ownership, not popularity. So D is maybe not correct. So
